questions.prompt: What is a Git branch, actually?

choices.text: A copy of all the code.

A movable pointer to a commit — literally a file in .git/refs/heads/ containing a single 40-character commit hash. Branches don't store code, don't store history, don't have any state beyond 'this hash.' The branch's apparent history is just whatever commits you can reach by walking parent pointers backward from that hash. Creating a branch is creating a one-line file — fast and cheap. Switching a branch moves HEAD (and the working dir) to that hash. This is why Git operations are so fast: most of them are just moving pointers around in a graph.

questions.explanation: A branch is just a file containing one commit hash. Cheap to create; just a pointer in the DAG.

questions.prompt: You've been making commits but realize you're not on any branch — `git status` says 'HEAD detached at a3f5b6c'. Now what?

choices.text: Create a branch at the current commit BEFORE switching away: `git switch -c new-branch-name`. The branch pointer is created at the current commit; your detached-HEAD commits are now on a named branch — safe and ready to push. If you've already switched away to another branch without saving, the reflog still has the commits for ~90 days: `git reflog` to find the HEAD@{N} where your work was; `git checkout HEAD@{N}` to return; then `git switch -c new-branch-name`. The reflog is Git's safety net — most 'lost' commits are actually recoverable from it.

questions.explanation: Create a branch with `git switch -c name` to save the commits. Or recover via reflog.
